column2: A system that allows for the sharing of identity information across multiple independent security domains or organizations.
The entity in a federation that authenticates the user and issues identity assertions/tokens.
The entity in a federation that provides the service or resource, relying on the IdP's authentication.
An XML-based framework for exchanging authentication and authorization data between an IdP and SP.
The XML document sent from an IdP to an SP containing statements about a user's identity and permissions.
An industry-standard protocol for delegated authorization (uses Access Tokens).
An authentication layer built on top of OAuth 2.0 (uses ID Tokens).
A mechanism allowing a user to authenticate once and gain access to multiple systems without re-authenticating.
The automatic creation of a user account in a service provider the first time a user logs in via federation.
A compact, URL-safe means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties; commonly used in OIDC.
An HTTP-based protocol designed to automate user provisioning and deprovisioning in cloud environments.
An XML file containing configuration info (endpoints, keys) used to establish trust between IdPs and SPs.
An extension to OAuth 2.0/OIDC that protects the authorization code flow against interception and injection attacks.
Parameters used in OIDC to request specific sets of user claims (e.g., profile, email).
A protected resource that returns claims about the authenticated user when presented with a valid access token.
A model where a central hub mediates trust between multiple identity providers and service providers.
A mechanism for sending SAML messages within the body of an HTML form using the POST method.
A mechanism for sending SAML messages as URL parameters in a GET request.
An element in an assertion that defines how a relying party can verify that the presenter is the subject.
A profile that allows a user to terminate all sessions across all service providers by logging out once.
An attack that manipulates the XML structure to bypass signature validation or change application logic.
The process of verifying a person's real-world identity before issuing digital credentials.
The most secure OAuth 2.0 flow for confidential clients, involving a back-channel token exchange.
An OAuth 2.0 flow used for machine-to-machine communication where no user is present.
A deprecated OAuth 2.0 flow that returns tokens directly in the URL fragment; replaced by Auth Code + PKCE.
